segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Jay Gim, a man in a blue and brown striped shirt, glasses, and a goatee, seated in a warmly lit room. He begins narrating a historical event from a dark winter night in 1945, as the scene transitions to a hand-drawn animation. We see a figure, identified as 14-year-old Billy Frank Jr., in a red coat by a nighttime riverbank, illuminated by flashlights from two silhouetted police officers. An officer's voice declares, 'You're under arrest!' to which Billy Frank Jr. animatedly responds, 'LEAVE ME ALONE! I LIVE!' raising his hands in surrender. The officers then escort him towards a police car, followed by a mugshot-style illustration of him holding a sign dated 'DEC 1945 FRANK POLICE DEPT TACOMA WASH.' Jay Gim explains that this arrest was an early battle in what became known as the Fish Wars. The video cuts back to Jay Gim, who introduces himself and welcomes viewers to 'Crash Course Native American History,' accompanied by upbeat intro music. The animation resumes, showing Billy Frank Jr. kneeling by the river, pulling a fishing net, as Jay Gim narrates that he was checking nets like his Nisqually Nation ancestors. A historical black-and-white photograph of 'Billy Frank Jr. fishing on the Nisqually River Washington (1973)' appears, showing him holding a large fish. Jay Gim continues, noting this was his first arrest but not his last, as he would be handcuffed over 50 more times. The video returns to Jay Gim, then to an animation of Billy Frank Jr. flanked by police officers, followed by a close-up of his face. The final animation depicts a diverse group of individuals, inspired by Billy Frank Jr.'s non-violent resistance, holding signs that read 'THIS LAND IS OUR LAND' and 'HONOR THE TREATIES,' with one person raising a fist in solidarity.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with a man in a blue and brown striped shirt, wearing glasses and a goatee, seated in a warmly lit room adorned with framed artwork and shelves holding books and candles. The scene transitions to a hand-drawn animation depicting a nighttime riverbank where a figure in a red coat is illuminated by a flashlight beam from two silhouetted officers. The figure raises their hands in surrender, and a speech bubble appears with the words 'LEAVE ME ALONE! I LIVE.' The officers escort the figure toward a police car, and the scene shifts to a mugshot-style illustration of the same person holding a sign reading 'DEC 1945 FRANK POLICE DEPT TACOMA WASH.' The video then cuts back to the man speaking, followed by a title card for 'Crash Course Native American History.' The animation resumes, showing the figure in the red coat kneeling by the river, pulling a fishing net from the water. A historical black-and-white photograph appears, captioned 'Billy Frank Jr. fishing on the Nisqually River Washington (1973),' showing a man holding a large fish. The video returns to the speaker, then to an animation of the man in the red coat flanked by two police officers, followed by a close-up of his face. The final animation shows a group of diverse individuals holding signs that read 'THIS LAND IS OUR LAND' and 'HONOR THE TREATIES,' with one person raising a fist in solidarity.

audio_updated_entities.Man in striped shirt: Jay Gim, a man with glasses, a goatee, and black ear piercings, wearing a blue and brown striped shirt, seated in a warmly lit room with framed artwork and shelves. He narrates historical events, quotes Billy Frank Jr., and introduces himself as the host of 'Crash Course Native American History'.
audio_updated_entities.Figure in red coat: A hand-drawn character with dark hair, wearing a red coat, identified as Billy Frank Jr. The audio reveals he was 14 years old in 1945 when he was arrested on the Nisqually River for checking fishing nets, shouting 'LEAVE ME ALONE! I LIVE!' He is depicted surrendering, being escorted by officers, in a mugshot, fishing, and later inspiring non-violent resistance. The audio notes he was a member of the Nisqually Nation and would be arrested over 50 more times.
audio_updated_entities.Police officers: Silhouetted and later blue-uniformed figures who arrest Billy Frank Jr. in 1945, with one officer heard saying, 'You're under arrest!' They are depicted escorting him and later flanking him in subsequent arrests.
A black-and-white photo from 1973 showing Billy Frank Jr. fishing on the Nisqually River, holding a large fish, reinforcing his identity and connection to the fishing rights struggle.
audio_updated_entities.Crash Course Native American History tit: A green textured title card with a diamond-shaped logo, introduced by host Jay Gim as the subject of the episode, accompanied by upbeat intro music.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a Man in a striped shirt, wearing glasses and a goatee, seated in a warmly lit room adorned with framed historical photographs and shelves of books and jars. He speaks directly to the camera, gesturing with a rolled paper, explaining that for Native people, food defines who they are. The scene transitions to a split-screen showing historical black-and-white photographs: on the left, Nisqually fisherman Johnny Bobb holding a large fish, and on the right, Nisqually Tribal Member Lou Ann Squally handling a fish in shallow water. The speaker notes the strong connection the Nisqually people had to fish, viewing salmon as gifts to be honored and protected, even if it meant facing jail time. This is immediately followed by a grainy historical image of arrests during the Fish Wars in Washington (1968–1970), depicting law enforcement officers detaining individuals. The video returns to the Man in the striped shirt, who continues speaking, introducing the concept of sovereignty. A split-screen graphic appears, defining 'Sovereignty' as 'The right of Native nations to govern themselves,' a definition he vocalizes. He then introduces 'Food Sovereignty,' defined on screen as 'The right of Native nations to use the foods that have sustained their communities since time immemorial,' also spoken aloud. The scene shifts to a close-up of hands holding a woven basket filled with colorful corn and herbs, representing traditional Indigenous foods, as the speaker elaborates on how Native communities had distinct, culturally defined relationships with their traditional, local, and seasonal foods before European arrival. This is followed by a display of dried corn cobs on a wooden surface. The video then shows Suquamish salmon preparation in Washington (2018), with salmon being smoked over a fire, before concluding with an illustrated calendar page for June, depicting strawberry plants, as the speaker mentions Algonquian tribes knew June as the 'strawberry moon.'
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with the Man in striped shirt seated in a warmly lit room adorned with framed historical photographs and shelves of books and jars, speaking directly to the camera. He gestures with a rolled paper, then the scene transitions to a split-screen showing historical black-and-white photographs: on the left, a Nisqually fisherman Johnny Bobb holding a fish, and on the right, Nisqually Tribal Member Lou Ann Squally handling a fish in shallow water. The next frame shows arrests during the Fish Wars in Washington (1968–1970), with law enforcement officers detaining individuals. The video returns to the Man in striped shirt, who continues speaking, now with a split-screen graphic defining 'Sovereignty' as 'The right of Native nations to govern themselves,' followed by 'Food Sovereignty' as 'The right of Native nations to use the foods that have sustained their communities since time immemorial.' The scene shifts to a close-up of hands holding a woven basket filled with traditional Indigenous foods, including colorful corn and herbs, then to a display of dried corn cobs on a wooden surface. The video then shows Suquamish salmon preparation in Washington (2018), with salmon being smoked over a fire. The final frame is an illustrated calendar page for June, depicting strawberry plants with flowers and fruit under a red sun.

audio_updated_entities.Historical photograph of Johnny Bobb: A black-and-white photo of a man holding a large fish, captioned 'Nisqually fisherman Johnny Bobb', framed with colorful geometric borders, shown as the speaker discusses the Nisqually people's connection to fish.
audio_updated_entities.Historical photograph of Lou Ann Squally: A black-and-white photo of a woman crouching in shallow water handling a fish, captioned 'Nisqually Tribal Member Lou Ann Squally', framed with colorful geometric borders, shown as the speaker discusses the Nisqually people's connection to fish.
audio_updated_entities.Arrests during the Fish Wars photograph: A grainy, colorized historical image showing law enforcement officers arresting individuals during the Fish Wars in Washington (1968–1970), framed with colorful geometric borders, visually illustrating the speaker's point about 'facing jail time' for protecting fish.
audio_updated_entities.Sovereignty definition graphic: A split-screen graphic with the Man in striped shirt on the left and a text box on the right defining 'Sovereignty' as 'The right of Native nations to govern themselves', set against a green textured background with colorful borders, with the definition being spoken aloud by the Man in striped shirt.
audio_updated_entities.Food Sovereignty definition graphic: A split-screen graphic with the Man in striped shirt on the left and a text box on the right defining 'Food Sovereignty' as 'The right of Native nations to use the foods that have sustained their communities since time immemorial', set against a green textured background with colorful borders, with the definition being spoken aloud by the Man in striped shirt.
audio_updated_entities.Traditional Indigenous foods basket: A close-up of hands holding a woven basket filled with colorful corn, herbs, and other traditional Indigenous foods, with a turquoise beaded necklace visible on the person’s chest, shown as the speaker describes the distinct, culturally defined relationships Native communities had with their traditional foods.
audio_updated_entities.Dried corn cobs display: A wooden surface displaying rows of dried corn cobs with multicolored kernels, alongside jars of beans and other grains, shown as the speaker describes the distinct, culturally defined relationships Native communities had with their traditional foods.
audio_updated_entities.Suquamish salmon preparation footage: A video clip showing salmon being smoked over a fire using a traditional wooden rack, captioned 'Suquamish salmon preparation Washington (2018)', framed with colorful geometric borders, shown as the speaker describes the distinct, culturally defined relationships Native communities had with their traditional foods.
audio_updated_entities.June calendar illustration: An illustrated calendar page for June, showing strawberry plants with green leaves, white flowers, and red strawberries, under a red sun and a blue sky with stars, drawn on lined paper, aligning with the speaker's mention of Algonquian tribes knowing June as the 'strawberry moon'.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with an illustrated calendar page for June, depicting strawberry plants under a red sun, which the narrator, a Man in a striped shirt, identifies as marking the beginning of berry season. This transitions to a September scene with pawpaw fruit and trees, which he explains the Shawnee knew as the 'Pawpaw Moon,' a time to enjoy the 'mango of the Midwest.' The Man in striped shirt, wearing glasses and a goatee, then speaks directly to the camera in a warmly lit room, explaining how Native peoples stock-piled foods. Historical black-and-white photographs and a close-up video clip illustrate the traditional acorn processing methods of the Southwestern Pomo Indians of California (c. 1923), showing baskets and hands grinding acorns to create flour by drying, shelling, smashing, and flushing out bitterness for use in soup, bread, or mush. The Man in striped shirt reappears, followed by an illustrated diagram of 'The Three Sisters'—corn, beans, and squash—growing together on graph paper, which he describes as crops that 'protected each other and grew better together,' as practiced by nations like the Haudenosaunee and Cherokee. The video then presents a map titled 'Native Land Dispossession,' showing pre-colonization Native land in orange, transitioning to a 'Present Day' map with a '99% Reduction' in orange areas, highlighting land lost to the U.S. in dark green. The Man in striped shirt concludes, emphasizing that 'all that changed when Europeans began colonizing' and that Native nations lost 99% of their lands over 'just over 300 years,' all set to a gentle, underlying instrumental score.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video begins with an illustrated calendar page for June, depicting strawberry plants under a red sun on lined paper, transitioning to a September scene with pawpaw fruit and trees under an orange sun. The scene shifts to the Man in striped shirt, who speaks directly to the camera in a warmly lit room with framed artwork and shelves in the background, holding a rolled paper. Historical black-and-white photographs appear, showing acorn storage and processing methods of the Southwestern Pomo Indians of California (c. 1923), including baskets and hands grinding acorns. The Man in striped shirt reappears, followed by an illustrated diagram of 'The Three Sisters'—corn, beans, and squash—growing together on graph paper. The video then presents a map titled 'Native Land Dispossession,' showing pre-colonization Native land in orange, which transitions to a 'Present Day' map with 99% reduction, highlighting land lost to the U.S. in dark green, with only fragmented orange areas remaining. The Man in striped shirt returns, emphasizing the narrative of land loss and cultural practices.

audio_updated_entities.Man in striped shirt: A man with glasses, a goatee, and black ear piercings, wearing a blue and brown striped shirt, seated in a warmly lit room with framed artwork and shelves, speaking directly to the camera in a clear, narrative voice, explaining traditional Native American practices, food cultivation, and the impact of colonization.
audio_updated_entities.June calendar illustration: An illustrated calendar page for June, showing strawberry plants under a red sun, identified by the narrator as marking the beginning of berry season.
audio_updated_entities.Historical photograph of acorn storage: A black-and-white photograph of two individuals beside a large woven acorn storage basket in California (c. 1923), part of a sequence illustrating the traditional acorn processing methods of Native peoples, specifically the Southwestern Pomo Indians, who created flour from raw acorns.
audio_updated_entities.Historical photograph of baskets used in: A black-and-white photograph showing baskets and acorns used in the preparation of acorn flour in California (c. 1923), part of a sequence illustrating the traditional acorn processing methods of Native peoples, specifically the Southwestern Pomo Indians, who created flour from raw acorns.
audio_updated_entities.Acorn processing methods of the Southwes: A close-up video clip showing hands grinding acorns in a bowl, with text overlay, illustrating the traditional processing methods of the Southwestern Pomo Indians of California, who created flour from raw acorns by drying, shelling, smashing, and flushing out bitterness for use in soup, bread, or mush.
audio_updated_entities.The Three Sisters illustration: An illustrated diagram on graph paper showing corn, beans, and squash growing together, labeled 'The Three Sisters,' explained by the narrator as crops that 'protected each other and grew better together,' as practiced by nations like the Haudenosaunee and Cherokee.
audio_updated_entities.Native Land Dispossession map: A series of maps titled 'Native Land Dispossession,' showing pre-colonization Native land in orange transitioning to a 'Present Day' map with a '99% Reduction' in orange areas, visually representing the land lost to the U.S. due to European colonization over 'just over 300 years,' as narrated.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a Man in striped shirt, wearing glasses and a goatee, speaking directly to the camera in a warmly lit room adorned with framed historical photographs, Native art, and shelves, as somber, reflective music plays. He explains that between the 19th and 20th centuries, the US government forced Native people off their lands, using federal boarding schools to eradicate their cultures. As he speaks, the scene transitions to historical black-and-white photographs, first showing numerous students in formal attire seated at long tables in the Carlisle Indian School dining hall (1901), then three women in aprons standing behind a table laden with food in the school's kitchen (c. 1895), and finally students tilling soil with tools in a garden (c. 1910). The Man in striped shirt continues, detailing how Native children were separated from their families, languages, traditions, and foods, often fed low-quality rations and punished for eating anything they'd grown while laboring on school farms. The video returns to the speaker, now with a visible 'YOU ARE ON NATIVE LAND' sign on the wall behind him, as he shifts to discussing how settlers exploited traditional food sources unsustainably. A colorful hand-drawn animation on lined paper then depicts a Native figure on a blue horse hunting buffalo, with teepees and other figures in traditional attire in the background, illustrating his point that the Great Sioux, or 'Buffalo Nation,' viewed buffalo as essential for survival – for food, clothing, shelter, and even their creation story. This is followed by a historical photograph of a buffalo herd grazing in Wyoming's Lamar Valley. The Man in striped shirt then describes how 30 million buffalo roamed the plains in the early 19th century, but as railroads expanded west, hunters on those trains killed them, leading to a historical photograph of the completion of the first transcontinental railroad in Utah (1869), showing a large group of men gathered around two trains with a handshake at the center.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video begins with the Man in striped shirt seated in a warmly lit room adorned with framed historical photographs, Native art, and shelves holding books and candles. He speaks directly to the camera, hands clasped, as the scene transitions to a series of historical black-and-white photographs. The first shows students in the dining hall at Carlisle Indian School in Pennsylvania (1901), seated at long tables in formal attire. This is followed by a photo of the kitchen at Carlisle Indian School (c. 1895), where three women in aprons stand behind a table laden with food. Next, students are shown working in a garden at Carlisle Indian School (c. 1910), tilling the soil with tools under the supervision of an adult. The video returns to the Man in striped shirt, who continues speaking, now with a visible 'YOU ARE ON NATIVE LAND' sign on the wall behind him. The scene shifts to a hand-drawn animation on lined paper, depicting a Native figure on a blue horse hunting buffalo, with teepees and other figures in traditional attire in the background. This transitions to a historical photograph of a buffalo herd in the Lamar Valley in Wyoming, followed by the completion of the first transcontinental railroad in Utah (1869), showing a large group of men gathered around two trains with a handshake at the center. Throughout, the visuals alternate between the speaker and historical imagery, creating a narrative about Native American history, education, and displacement.

active_entities.Man in striped shirt: A man with glasses, a goatee, and black ear piercings, wearing a blue and brown striped shirt, seated in a room with framed artwork and shelves in the background, speaking and gesturing with a rolled paper. The background now includes a visible 'YOU ARE ON NATIVE LAND' sign.

audio_updated_entities.Man in striped shirt: A man with glasses, a goatee, and black ear piercings, wearing a blue and brown striped shirt, seated in a warmly lit room with framed artwork, Native art, and shelves, speaking directly to the camera about the US government's forced removal of Native people, the eradication of their cultures through federal boarding schools, the separation of children from families and traditions, the exploitation of traditional food sources, and the historical significance and subsequent decline of buffalo due to westward expansion and railroad hunters. A 'YOU ARE ON NATIVE LAND' sign is visible behind him during part of his speech.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a stark 1892 historical photograph of a massive pile of bison skulls in Michigan, men standing at its base and one atop the mound, illustrating the scale of bison extermination. The Man in striped shirt, seated in a warmly lit room with framed artwork and shelves, speaks directly to the camera, explaining that by 1887, fewer than 100 individual buffalo remained in the wild, a deliberate act that eliminated a traditional food source and destroyed the way of life for groups like the Great Sioux. A quote from a U.S. Army soldier from 1867 appears on screen, stating, 'Kill every buffalo you can. Every buffalo dead is an Indian gone,' reinforcing the speaker's point that the US government knew this would force Native Americans to sign treaties and cede land. This is followed by a sepia-toned 1883 illustration titled 'The End' by Martin S. Garretson, depicting a desolate landscape littered with bison bones, a wagon, and a lone figure, symbolizing the aftermath. The Man in striped shirt reappears, then a map of Washington State is shown, marking the Nisqually Reservation and the approximate ceded Nisqually territory, as the speaker transitions to discussing Billy Frank Jr. and the Fish Wars. He explains that in the mid-19th century, the Nisqually and other Native Nations, who identified as 'salmon people,' signed treaties with the US government, ceding millions of acres in Washington State, but under the big condition that they would keep the right to fish. A close-up of the 1854 Treaty with the Nisqually, Puyallup, etc. follows, with Article 3 highlighted: 'The right of taking fish, at all usual and accustomed grounds and stations, is further secured to said Indians,' underscoring these treaty-protected fishing rights. The segment concludes with a 1905 photograph of men posing with a massive spruce log in the Cascade Mountains, symbolizing resource exploitation, with the Man in striped shirt briefly reappearing to continue his narration about the decades of challenges.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video begins with a historical photograph from 1892 showing a massive pile of bison skulls in Michigan, with men standing at its base and one atop the mound, emphasizing the scale of bison extermination. The scene transitions to the Man in striped shirt, seated in a warmly lit room with framed artwork and shelves, speaking directly to the camera. A quote from a U.S. Army soldier in 1867 appears on screen: 'Kill every buffalo you can. Every buffalo dead is an Indian gone,' highlighting the deliberate targeting of bison to undermine Native American livelihoods. This is followed by a sepia-toned 1883 illustration titled 'The End' by Martin S. Garretson, depicting a desolate landscape littered with bison bones, a wagon, and a lone figure, symbolizing the aftermath of the bison’s near-extinction. The Man in striped shirt reappears, then a map of Washington State is shown, marking the Nisqually Reservation and the approximate ceded Nisqually territory, illustrating land loss. A close-up of the 1854 Treaty with the Nisqually, Puyallup, etc. follows, with Article 3 highlighted: 'The right of taking fish, at all usual and accustomed grounds and stations, is further secured to said Indians,' underscoring treaty-protected fishing rights. The segment concludes with a 1905 photograph of men posing with a massive spruce log in the Cascade Mountains, symbolizing the exploitation of natural resources, with the Man in striped shirt briefly reappearing to continue his narration.

active_entities.Man in striped shirt: A man with glasses, a goatee, and black ear piercings, wearing a blue and brown striped shirt, seated in a room with framed artwork and shelves in the background, speaking and gesturing with a rolled paper. The background now includes a visible 'YOU ARE ON NATIVE LAND' sign.

audio_updated_entities.Man in striped shirt: A man with glasses, a goatee, and black ear piercings, wearing a blue and brown striped shirt, seated in a warmly lit room with framed artwork and shelves in the background, speaking directly to the camera and narrating the historical context of bison extermination and Native American treaties, with a visible 'YOU ARE ON NATIVE LAND' sign in the background.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a series of historical black-and-white photographs illustrating early 20th-century salmon exploitation, beginning with cannery workers at Apex Fish Co. in 1914, two women in aprons and hairnets standing by a table of canned fish, emphasizing industrial processing. This transitions to a 1902 image of a massive pile of fifty tons of salmon caught in Puget Sound, showcasing the scale of commercial fishing, followed by a 1906 photograph of commercial fishermen laboriously unloading salmon from a net onto a dock. The Man in striped shirt's voice begins, explaining how logging, mining, commercial fishing, and other man-made activities led to the salmon population collapsing by the mid-20th century, much like the buffalo before them. The visuals then shift to the 1968–1970 period, with a colorized image of law enforcement patrolling riverbanks, followed by Native fishers practicing their treaty rights, including a close-up of a fisher in a boat with a 'FISH FIGHT' sign. The Man in striped shirt continues, detailing how state laws meant to protect fish led to law enforcement disproportionately cracking down on Native fishers on riverbanks, who took less than 5% of the annual catch, rather than offshore commercial boats. He then appears directly to the camera in a warmly lit room adorned with framed artwork, shelves, and a 'YOU ARE ON NATIVE LAND' sign, recounting how Billy Frank Jr. was first arrested for fishing, an act he initially saw as a matter of sustenance, not politics. Historical images of Billy Frank Jr. at a congressional hearing in Washington D.C. and Muckleshoot fishers in the Green River in 1970 are shown as the speaker explains that Frank Jr. and other Native fishers understood their treaty rights, knowing their exercise was legal under the U.S. government but illegal under state law, and crucial for their health, cultural identity, and survival, ultimately making their actions profoundly political. The segment concludes with the Man in striped shirt continuing his narration, linking these past struggles to present-day sovereignty and food rights.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video begins with a series of historical black-and-white photographs depicting early 20th-century salmon fishing and canning operations in Washington. The first image shows cannery workers at Apex Fish Co. in 1914, with two women in aprons and hairnets standing beside a long table filled with rows of canned fish, emphasizing industrial food processing. This transitions to a 1902 photograph of fifty tons of salmon caught in Puget Sound, showcasing a massive pile of fish, highlighting the scale of commercial fishing. Next, a 1906 image shows commercial fishermen unloading salmon from a net onto a dock, illustrating the labor-intensive process of harvesting. The sequence then shifts to the 1968–1970 period, with a colorized image of law enforcement patrolling riverbanks, followed by Native fishers practicing their treaty rights, including a close-up of a fisher in a boat with a 'FISH FIGHT' sign, symbolizing resistance. The narrative then moves to a modern setting, where the Man in striped shirt speaks directly to the camera in a warmly lit room adorned with framed artwork, shelves, and a 'YOU ARE ON NATIVE LAND' sign, providing context and commentary. Historical images of Billy Frank Jr. at a congressional hearing in Washington D.C. and Muckleshoot fishers in the Green River in 1970 are shown, underscoring the legal and cultural struggle for fishing rights. The segment concludes with the Man in striped shirt continuing his narration, linking past struggles to present-day sovereignty and food rights.

active_entities.Man in striped shirt: A man with glasses, a goatee, and black ear piercings, wearing a blue and brown striped shirt, seated in a room with framed artwork and shelves in the background, speaking and gesturing with a rolled paper. The background now includes a visible 'YOU ARE ON NATIVE LAND' sign.
A black-and-white photo of Billy Frank Jr. (center) at a congressional hearing in Washington D.C., seated at a table with microphones, wearing a light-colored suit, surrounded by other attendees in formal attire.
active_entities.Arrests during the Fish Wars photograph: A grainy, colorized historical image showing law enforcement officers arresting individuals during the Fish Wars in Washington (1968–1970), framed with colorful geometric borders, depicting tension and conflict over fishing rights.

audio_updated_entities.Man in striped shirt: A man with glasses, a goatee, and black ear piercings, wearing a blue and brown striped shirt, seated in a warmly lit room with framed artwork, shelves, and a 'YOU ARE ON NATIVE LAND' sign, speaks directly to the camera, explaining how logging, mining, and commercial fishing led to salmon population collapse by the mid-20th century, and how discriminatory state laws disproportionately targeted Native fishers on riverbanks, leading to arrests like that of Frank Jr., who initially fished for sustenance but whose actions became deeply political as he and other Native fishers asserted their treaty rights for health, cultural identity, and survival.
A black-and-white photo of Billy Frank Jr. (center) at a congressional hearing in Washington D.C., seated at a table with microphones, wearing a light-colored suit, surrounded by other attendees in formal attire, is shown as the speaker recounts how Frank Jr. was first arrested for fishing, an act he initially viewed as a means of survival rather than a political statement, but which became central to the assertion of treaty rights.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a series of black-and-white historical photographs, as a male narrator's voice declares, "So the fish wars began." The first image shows Billy Frank Jr. and his family being confronted by law enforcement in 1970, with a man in a long coat pointing authoritatively. The narrator explains that by the 1960s, Frank Jr. and other Native activists were engaging in non-violent civil disobedience, staging demonstrations like fish-ins. This is visually supported by a photo of activist Al Bridges speaking into a bullhorn at a fish-in. The narrator continues, detailing the sabotage, violence, and clashes with state police faced by Native fishermen, coinciding with a photo of law enforcement vehicles and officers gathering to arrest protestors. The movement gained nationwide attention, drawing support from celebrities like Marlon Brando, who is seen in a photo with Puyallup activist Bob Satiacum fishing on the Puyallup River in 1964. The narrator then highlights Billy Frank Jr.'s frequent arrests, quoting him: "'I was not a policy guy... I was a getting-arrested guy.'" The video transitions to a modern-day interview with the Man in striped shirt, who speaks directly to the camera in a warmly lit room adorned with artwork and a 'YOU ARE ON NATIVE LAND' sign. A graphic displaying Billy Frank Jr.'s quote appears. The narrator explains that in 1974, Frank Jr. and other activists finally had their day in court, leading to the landmark 'Boldt Decision.' A graphic explaining 'The Boldt Decision' appears beside the Man in striped shirt, detailing the 1974 ruling that upheld treaty rights and granted Native nations half the harvestable fish catch. An air horn sound effect punctuates the narrator's excitement for this "huge win." The Man in striped shirt continues speaking, crossing his arms with a serious demeanor, as the narrator concludes by emphasizing that food sovereignty cannot be separated from rights.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video segment begins with a series of black-and-white historical photographs depicting key moments in the Native American fishing rights movement in Washington during the 1960s and 1970s. The first image shows Billy Frank Jr. and his family being confronted by law enforcement in 1970, with a man in a long coat pointing authoritatively while others stand nearby in a wooded area. This is followed by a photo of activist Al Bridges speaking into a bullhorn at a fish-in, emphasizing the protest’s public nature. Next, law enforcement vehicles and officers are shown gathering to arrest protestors, highlighting the tension and conflict. A photograph of actor Marlon Brando and Puyallup activist Bob Satiacum fishing on the Puyallup River in 1964 underscores the cultural and political significance of the issue. The segment then transitions to a modern-day interview with the Man in striped shirt, who speaks directly to the camera in a warmly lit room adorned with framed artwork, a 'YOU ARE ON NATIVE LAND' sign, and shelves with books and candles. He gestures expressively as he discusses the movement, and a graphic appears beside him explaining 'The Boldt Decision'—the 1974 court ruling that upheld treaty rights and granted Native nations half the harvestable fish catch. A quote from Billy Frank Jr. is displayed: '[I] was not a policy guy... I was a getting-arrested guy,' reinforcing the grassroots, confrontational nature of the struggle. The Man in striped shirt continues speaking, crossing his arms and maintaining a serious demeanor, as the segment concludes with a focus on the legal and personal dimensions of the fight for treaty rights.

active_entities.Man in striped shirt: A man with glasses, a goatee, and black ear piercings, wearing a blue and brown striped shirt, seated in a room with framed artwork and shelves in the background, speaking and gesturing with a rolled paper. The background now includes a visible 'YOU ARE ON NATIVE LAND' sign.
A black-and-white photo of Billy Frank Jr. (center) at a congressional hearing in Washington D.C., seated at a table with microphones, wearing a light-colored suit, surrounded by other attendees in formal attire.

audio_updated_entities.Man in striped shirt: A man with glasses, a goatee, and black ear piercings, wearing a blue and brown striped shirt, seated in a warmly lit room with framed artwork, a 'YOU ARE ON NATIVE LAND' sign, and shelves with books and candles. He speaks directly to the camera, gesturing expressively, and later crosses his arms, maintaining a serious demeanor, while a male narrator's voice discusses the Native American fishing rights movement, the Boldt Decision, and food sovereignty.
A black-and-white photo from 1970 showing Billy Frank Jr. (center) and his family being confronted by law enforcement in a wooded area, with a man in a long coat pointing authoritatively, as the male narrator describes the beginning of the 'fish wars' and non-violent acts of civil disobedience.
audio_updated_entities.Historical photograph of Al Bridges: A black-and-white photo of activist Al Bridges speaking into a bullhorn at a fish-in in Washington (1970), wearing a headband and traditional attire, standing on a vehicle with trees in the background, as the male narrator mentions fish-ins where treaty rights were asserted.
audio_updated_entities.Historical photograph of law enforcement: A black-and-white photo showing police vehicles and officers gathering to arrest protestors in Washington (1970), with 'POLICE' written on a car, as the male narrator describes clashes with state police and violence faced by Native fishermen.
audio_updated_entities.Historical photograph of Marlon Brando a: A black-and-white photo of actor Marlon Brando (left) and Puyallup activist Bob Satiacum holding fish on the Puyallup River in Washington (1964), with Brando smiling, as the male narrator notes the movement attracted nationwide attention and support from celebrities like Brando.
audio_updated_entities.The Boldt Decision graphic: A split-screen graphic with the Man in striped shirt on the left and a text box on the right defining 'The Boldt Decision' as the 1974 court ruling that upheld Treaty Rights and ruled that Native nations in Washington were entitled to half of the harvestable catch of fish, set against a dark textured background with colorful borders, while the male narrator explains this landmark decision.
audio_updated_entities.Billy Frank Jr. quote graphic: A graphic displaying the quote: '[I] was not a policy guy... I was a getting-arrested guy.' attributed to Billy Frank Jr., shown alongside a portrait of him in a white shirt with a turquoise necklace, framed with colorful borders, as the male narrator quotes him and describes his frequent arrests.
audio_updated_entities.Narrator (male voice): A male voice that describes the historical context of the Native American fishing rights movement, the 'fish wars,' non-violent acts of civil disobedience, the involvement of activists like Billy Frank Jr. and Marlon Brando, the legal battles leading to the Boldt Decision, and the concept of food sovereignty. The narrator also makes an air horn sound effect.

segments.integrated_caption: Against a backdrop of warm lighting, framed artwork, and a 'YOU ARE ON NATIVE LAND' sign, a Man in a striped shirt speaks directly to the camera, gesturing with a rolled paper, explaining how disruptions to traditional food systems, like those experienced by the Great Sioux and Nisqually, impact the health and well-being of Native communities. He emphasizes that every Native nation in the US has faced such disruptions, leading to a common story of separation and resilience, underscored by a subtle, reflective instrumental track. He then introduces frybread as an example, describing it as a 'delectable, deep-fried disc of flour, salt, water, and baking powder' and a 'staple at powwows,' as visuals of golden-brown frybread appear, first plain, then topped with lettuce, tomatoes, and cheese. The Man details frybread's 'complicated past,' tracing its origins to the 1860s when the US government forced thousands of Navajo Nation members from their homelands. Historical black-and-white photographs of Navajo people on the Bosque Redondo Indian Reservation and a map illustrating the 'nearly 500-kilometer journey' to the reservation in New Mexico appear, as he recounts how 'hundreds died' and survivors found the land unsuitable for traditional crops like 'corn, beans, and squash,' leading to government rations of white flour. The segment concludes with modern footage from Wyoming (2019) showing ingredients for frybread—flour, nonfat dry milk, salt, and baking powder—being mixed in a kitchen, visually connecting the historical context of survival and adaptation to the contemporary cultural practice of making frybread.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video begins with the Man in striped shirt seated in a warmly lit room adorned with framed artwork, shelves holding books and candles, and a visible 'YOU ARE ON NATIVE LAND' sign. He speaks directly to the camera with his arms crossed, then gestures with a rolled paper as he explains a topic. The scene transitions to a close-up of golden-brown frybread on a blue and white patterned plate, labeled 'Frybread,' followed by a second image of frybread topped with lettuce, tomatoes, and cheese, served outdoors with a red drink. The narrative shifts to historical black-and-white photographs: first, Navajo people on the Bosque Redondo Indian Reservation in New Mexico (c. 1863–1868), then a map illustrating the forced relocation from Navajo Homelands to the Bosque Redondo Reservation and Internment Camp. Another historical photo shows corn fields at the same reservation. The video then cuts to a modern kitchen scene in Wyoming (2019), where ingredients for frybread—flour, nonfat dry milk, salt, and baking powder—are displayed on a counter. A woman begins mixing the ingredients in a blue bowl, with the text 'Making frybread on the Wind River Indian Reservation Wyoming (2019)' and the Wyoming PBS logo visible. The sequence connects the historical context of displacement and survival with the contemporary practice of making frybread as a cultural and culinary act of resilience.

active_entities.Man in striped shirt: A man with glasses, a goatee, and black ear piercings, wearing a blue and brown striped shirt, seated in a room with framed artwork and shelves in the background, speaking and gesturing with a rolled paper. The background now includes a visible 'YOU ARE ON NATIVE LAND' sign.

audio_updated_entities.Man in striped shirt: A man with glasses, a goatee, and black ear piercings, wearing a blue and brown striped shirt, seated in a warmly lit room with framed artwork, shelves, and a visible 'YOU ARE ON NATIVE LAND' sign. He speaks directly to the camera, gesturing with a rolled paper, explaining how disruptions to traditional food systems impact Native nations, leading to stories of separation and resilience. He specifically details the history of frybread, linking its origins to the forced relocation of the Navajo Nation in the 1860s, the hardships faced on the Bosque Redondo Reservation, and the introduction of government rations like white flour.
audio_updated_entities.Historical photograph of Navajo on the B: A black-and-white photograph showing Navajo people gathered around horses and buildings on the Bosque Redondo Indian Reservation in New Mexico (c. 1863–1868), framed with colorful geometric borders. This image illustrates the forced relocation of the Navajo Nation, a journey where 'hundreds died' to a reservation in what's now New Mexico.
audio_updated_entities.Map of Navajo Homelands and Bosque Redon: A green-toned map showing the Navajo Homelands in orange, with a red wavy line indicating the forced relocation to the Bosque Redondo Reservation and Internment Camp in New Mexico, framed with colorful geometric borders. This map visually represents the 'nearly 500-kilometer journey' mentioned by the speaker.
audio_updated_entities.Historical photograph of Corn Fields - B: A black-and-white photograph depicting Navajo people working in corn fields on the Bosque Redondo Indian Reservation in New Mexico (c. 1863–1868), framed with colorful geometric borders. This image illustrates the challenges faced by survivors who found the land 'wasn't good for growing their traditional staples of corn, beans, and squash.'
audio_updated_entities.Frybread: Golden-brown, puffy fried bread shown on a blue and white patterned plate, and later topped with lettuce, tomatoes, cheese, and meat on a white plate outdoors, with the label 'Frybread' displayed in a beige text box. Aurally, it is described by the speaker as 'a delectable, deep-fried disc of flour, salt, water, and baking powder that's a staple at powwows,' with a 'complicated past' tracing back to the 1860s and the Navajo Nation's forced relocation.
audio_updated_entities.Wyoming PBS footage of making frybread: A modern kitchen scene showing ingredients for frybread (flour, nonfat dry milk, salt, baking powder) on a wooden counter, with a woman mixing them in a blue bowl. Text overlay reads 'Making frybread on the Wind River Indian Reservation Wyoming (2019)' and the Wyoming PBS logo is visible. This footage visually demonstrates the contemporary practice of making frybread, using ingredients like 'white flour' that became part of the diet due to historical circumstances.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of hands mixing ingredients in a blue bowl, part of Wyoming PBS footage showing frybread being made on the Wind River Indian Reservation in 2019, followed by a shot of golden-brown frybread being lifted from a hot oil pan. A man in a striped shirt, seated in a warmly lit room with a 'YOU ARE ON NATIVE LAND' sign, speaks directly to the camera, explaining that frybread, made from flour, sugar, and lard, became a staple for Native Nations given similar rations. He notes that while some today see frybread as a reminder of traditional food systems being torn apart, contributing to high rates of diabetes and heart disease, others view it as a symbol of comfort and resilience, making something from nothing to feed everybody. Gentle, uplifting music plays in the background as the man continues, stating that this same resilience, which ignited the 'fish wars' and brought frybread, is evident in numerous native food initiatives across the U.S. The scene transitions to still images: a 'Navajo Taco' stand, a crowded 'Dine Cafe', and a person harvesting huckleberries in Washington in 2022, with a close-up of the berries in a woven basket. The man's narration highlights the Muckleshoot Food Sovereignty Project, which hosts harvest festivals, maintains community gardens, and teaches tribal members to prepare salmon and elk, coinciding with visuals of smoked salmon hanging in a smokehouse from Cherokee traditional salmon smoking in Washington (2023). He then mentions the Cherokee Nation sharing free heirloom seed packets with members to encourage growing ancestral crops, shown as bags of seeds from the Cherokee Nation Heirloom Seed Program in Oklahoma (2016). The segment concludes with a woman in a blue jacket in a field at the 'Three Sisters Project at Iowa State University Iowa', with close-ups of corn, beans, and squash growing together, as the man explains this project partners with native gardeners to study these traditional agricultural practices.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with a close-up of hands mixing ingredients in a blue bowl, labeled 'Making frybread on the Wind River Indian Reservation Wyoming (2019)', followed by a shot of golden-brown frybread being lifted from a hot oil pan. The scene transitions to the Man in striped shirt, seated in a warmly lit room with framed artwork and shelves, speaking directly to the camera. The video then cuts to a series of still images framed with colorful borders: a 'Navajo Taco' stand with a customer ordering, a crowded 'Dine Cafe' with people eating outdoors, and a person harvesting huckleberries in Washington (2022), with a close-up of the berries in a woven basket. Next, a shot of smoked salmon hanging in a smokehouse appears, labeled 'Cherokee traditional salmon smoking Washington (2023)', followed by bags of seeds from the 'Cherokee Nation Heirloom Seed Program Oklahoma (2016)'. The final scenes show a woman in a blue jacket standing in a field at the 'Three Sisters Project at Iowa State University Iowa', with close-ups of corn, beans, and squash growing together in the soil, emphasizing traditional agricultural practices.

active_entities.Man in striped shirt: A man with glasses, a goatee, and black ear piercings, wearing a blue and brown striped shirt, seated in a room with framed artwork and shelves in the background, speaking directly to the camera. The background now includes a visible 'YOU ARE ON NATIVE LAND' sign.
active_entities.Wyoming PBS footage of making frybread: A modern kitchen scene showing ingredients for frybread (flour, nonfat dry milk, salt, baking powder) on a wooden counter, with a woman mixing them in a blue bowl. Text overlay reads 'Making frybread on the Wind River Indian Reservation Wyoming (2019)' and the Wyoming PBS logo is visible.
active_entities.Frybread: Golden-brown, puffy fried bread shown being lifted from a hot oil pan, with a close-up of its crispy texture and golden surface.

audio_updated_entities.Man in striped shirt: A man with glasses, a goatee, and black ear piercings, wearing a blue and brown striped shirt, seated in a room with framed artwork and shelves in the background, speaking directly to the camera about the history and cultural significance of frybread, native food initiatives, and specific tribal projects like the Muckleshoot Food Sovereignty Project, Cherokee Nation Heirloom Seed Program, and the Three Sisters Project. The background now includes a visible 'YOU ARE ON NATIVE LAND' sign.
audio_updated_entities.Frybread: Golden-brown, puffy fried bread shown being lifted from a hot oil pan, with a close-up of its crispy texture and golden surface. The man speaking explains it was made from flour, sugar, and lard due to rations, and is seen by some as a reminder of broken traditional food systems leading to health issues, while others view it as a symbol of comfort and resilience.
audio_updated_entities.Muckleshoot huckleberry harvest Washingt: A person in a red shirt and woven hat harvesting huckleberries in a forest, with a close-up of the dark purple and red berries in a woven basket. This visual is contextualized by the speaker mentioning the Muckleshoot Food Sovereignty Project, which hosts harvest festivals, maintains community gardens, and teaches tribal members how to prepare salmon and elk.
audio_updated_entities.Cherokee traditional salmon smoking Wash: Smoked salmon strips hanging in a smokehouse, with a text overlay indicating the location and year. This visual is linked to the Muckleshoot Food Sovereignty Project's efforts to teach tribal members how to prepare salmon.
audio_updated_entities.Cherokee Nation Heirloom Seed Program Ok: Clear plastic bags labeled 'Cherokee White Eagle Corn' and other heirloom seeds, with the Cherokee Nation Administration Support Dept. address printed on the bags. The speaker explains that the Cherokee Nation shares these free heirloom seed packets with their members, encouraging them to grow and eat the same crops as their ancestors.
audio_updated_entities.Three Sisters Project at Iowa State Univ: A woman in a blue jacket and black pants standing in a field observing rows of corn, beans, and squash growing together, with close-ups of the plants in the soil. The speaker describes this as the Three Sisters Project at Iowa State University, which partners with native gardeners to study how growing corn, beans, and squash together works.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a person's ear and hair, identified by on-screen text as Shawn Sherman, 'The Sioux Chef,' set against a backdrop of background music. The scene transitions to a group of Native American chefs in white chef uniforms preparing Indigenous cuisine for the White House Tribal Nations Summit in Washington, D.C. (2023), with a woman in a blazer standing beside them, as the narration highlights how Native chefs are making their mark in the culinary world by opening restaurants, publishing cookbooks, and bringing traditional foods into the limelight. This leads into a cooking demonstration titled 'Indigikitchen with Mariah Gladstone,' showing the preparation of pemmican: blending super dry meat into powder, adding wild rice, pressing into patties, and frying. The narrator identifies Mariah Gladstone as a member of the Blackfeet and Cherokee Nations and the host of Indigikitchen, an online cooking show, who shares traditional food knowledge, teaching viewers how to make pemmican and balsamic manoomin with dandelion greens. A man with glasses, a goatee, and black ear piercings, wearing a blue and brown striped shirt, appears seated in a warmly lit room with framed artwork and shelves, speaking directly to the camera, explaining that for thousands of years, Native Nations hunted, fished, grew, and gathered food that sustained their communities, but under colonization, they faced violent disconnection from those food systems. The video then cuts to a buffet of traditional Indigenous foods in Alaska (2016), including a display of dried fish and a plate with a bowl of soup and a side of greens, followed by a scene of Suquamish salmon preparation in Washington (2018), with salmon being smoked over a fire. The narrator continues, emphasizing that Native people recognize that nourishing a community supports its ability to exist, leading many Native Nations to fight to keep traditional foods in their communities. The segment concludes with an aerial view of a dam on the Nisqually River, as the narrator states that the salmon in the Nisqually River still face threats.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a person's ear and hair, identified by on-screen text as Shawn Sherman, 'The Sioux Chef,' suggesting a focus on Indigenous culinary expertise. The scene transitions to a group of Native American chefs in white chef uniforms preparing Indigenous cuisine for the White House Tribal Nations Summit in Washington, D.C. (2023), with a woman in a blazer standing beside them. The narrative then shifts to a cooking demonstration titled 'Indigikitchen with Mariah Gladstone,' showing the preparation of pemmican: blending super dry meat into powder, adding wild rice, pressing into patties, and frying. A man with glasses, a goatee, and black ear piercings, wearing a blue and brown striped shirt, appears seated in a warmly lit room with framed artwork and shelves, speaking directly to the camera. The video then cuts to a buffet of traditional Indigenous foods in Alaska (2016), including a display of dried fish and a plate with a bowl of soup and a side of greens. This is followed by a scene of Suquamish salmon preparation in Washington (2018), with salmon being smoked over a fire. The segment concludes with an aerial view of a dam on the Nisqually River, emphasizing the environmental and cultural significance of waterways to Indigenous communities.

active_entities.Man in striped shirt: A man with glasses, a goatee, and black ear piercings, wearing a blue and brown striped shirt, seated in a room with framed artwork and shelves, speaking directly to the camera. The background includes a visible 'YOU ARE ON NATIVE LAND' sign.

audio_updated_entities.Man in striped shirt: A man with glasses, a goatee, and black ear piercings, wearing a blue and brown striped shirt, seated in a warmly lit room with framed artwork and shelves, speaking directly to the camera, serving as the primary narrator for this segment, discussing Native chefs' impact, Mariah Gladstone's work, the historical context of Indigenous food systems, the effects of colonization, and the current challenges faced by the Nisqually River.
audio_updated_entities.Indigikitchen with Mariah Gladstone: A cooking demonstration showing the steps to make pemmican: blending super dry meat into powder, adding wild rice, pressing into patties, and frying, with on-screen text guiding each step. This demonstration is hosted by Mariah Gladstone, identified by the narrator as a member of the Blackfeet and Cherokee Nations and the host of Indigikitchen, an online cooking show, who shares traditional food knowledge, teaching viewers how to make pemmican and balsamic manoomin with dandelion greens.
audio_updated_entities.Dam on the Nisqually River: An aerial view of a large concrete dam spanning the Nisqually River, surrounded by green trees and a road, which the narrator explicitly states still poses threats to the salmon, highlighting its ongoing environmental and cultural impact on Indigenous communities.

segments.integrated_caption: A male speaker explains that dams, habitat destruction, and overharvesting are issues, but the state of Washington now co-manages the river and fish alongside the Nisqually and other Native Nations. He then highlights Billy Frank Jr.'s continued activism in protecting tribal treaty rights until his death in 2014, noting that a year later he was posthumously awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom and had a national wildlife refuge named in his honor. The speaker then announces that the next episode will delve into Native Americans' first contact with Europeans, before thanking viewers for watching this episode of Crash Course Native American History, which was filmed at their studio in Indianapolis, Indiana, and made with the help of many people. He concludes by inviting viewers to join their community on Patreon to support keeping Crash Course free, all while upbeat, slightly melancholic background music plays, becoming more prominent towards the end.

audio_updated_entities.State of Washington: The state that co-manages the river and fish alongside Native Nations.
audio_updated_entities.Nisqually and other Native Nations: Groups that co-manage the river and fish alongside the state of Washington.
An individual who remained active in protecting tribe's treaty rights until his death in 2014, was awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom posthumously, and had a national wildlife refuge named in his honor.
audio_updated_entities.Crash Course Native American History: The name of the educational series being presented.
audio_updated_entities.Indianapolis, Indiana: The location of the studio where the episode was filmed.
audio_updated_entities.Patreon: A platform where viewers can join a community to support the show.

entity_library.Man in striped shirt: A man with glasses, a goatee, and black ear piercings, wearing a blue and brown striped shirt, seated in a warmly lit room with framed artwork and shelves, speaking directly to the camera, serving as the primary narrator for this segment, discussing Native chefs' impact, Mariah Gladstone's work, the historical context of Indigenous food systems, the effects of colonization, and the current challenges faced by the Nisqually River.
entity_library.Figure in red coat: A hand-drawn character with dark hair, wearing a red coat, identified as Billy Frank Jr. The audio reveals he was 14 years old in 1945 when he was arrested on the Nisqually River for checking fishing nets, shouting 'LEAVE ME ALONE! I LIVE!' He is depicted surrendering, being escorted by officers, in a mugshot, fishing, and later inspiring non-violent resistance. The audio notes he was a member of the Nisqually Nation and would be arrested over 50 more times.
entity_library.Police officers: Silhouetted and later blue-uniformed figures who arrest Billy Frank Jr. in 1945, with one officer heard saying, 'You're under arrest!' They are depicted escorting him and later flanking him in subsequent arrests.
A black-and-white photo of Billy Frank Jr. (center) at a congressional hearing in Washington D.C., seated at a table with microphones, wearing a light-colored suit, surrounded by other attendees in formal attire, is shown as the speaker recounts how Frank Jr. was first arrested for fishing, an act he initially viewed as a means of survival rather than a political statement, but which became central to the assertion of treaty rights.
entity_library.Crash Course Native American History tit: A green textured title card with a diamond-shaped logo, introduced by host Jay Gim as the subject of the episode, accompanied by upbeat intro music.
entity_library.Historical photograph of Johnny Bobb: A black-and-white photo of a man holding a large fish, captioned 'Nisqually fisherman Johnny Bobb', framed with colorful geometric borders, shown as the speaker discusses the Nisqually people's connection to fish.
entity_library.Historical photograph of Lou Ann Squally: A black-and-white photo of a woman crouching in shallow water handling a fish, captioned 'Nisqually Tribal Member Lou Ann Squally', framed with colorful geometric borders, shown as the speaker discusses the Nisqually people's connection to fish.
entity_library.Arrests during the Fish Wars photograph: A grainy, colorized historical image showing law enforcement officers arresting individuals during the Fish Wars in Washington (1968–1970), framed with colorful geometric borders, visually illustrating the speaker's point about 'facing jail time' for protecting fish.
entity_library.Sovereignty definition graphic: A split-screen graphic with the Man in striped shirt on the left and a text box on the right defining 'Sovereignty' as 'The right of Native nations to govern themselves', set against a green textured background with colorful borders, with the definition being spoken aloud by the Man in striped shirt.
entity_library.Food Sovereignty definition graphic: A split-screen graphic with the Man in striped shirt on the left and a text box on the right defining 'Food Sovereignty' as 'The right of Native nations to use the foods that have sustained their communities since time immemorial', set against a green textured background with colorful borders, with the definition being spoken aloud by the Man in striped shirt.
entity_library.Traditional Indigenous foods basket: A close-up of hands holding a woven basket filled with colorful corn, herbs, and other traditional Indigenous foods, with a turquoise beaded necklace visible on the person’s chest, shown as the speaker describes the distinct, culturally defined relationships Native communities had with their traditional foods.
entity_library.Dried corn cobs display: A wooden surface displaying rows of dried corn cobs with multicolored kernels, alongside jars of beans and other grains, shown as the speaker describes the distinct, culturally defined relationships Native communities had with their traditional foods.
entity_library.Suquamish salmon preparation footage: A video clip showing salmon being smoked over a fire using a traditional wooden rack, captioned 'Suquamish salmon preparation Washington (2018)', framed with colorful geometric borders, shown as the speaker describes the distinct, culturally defined relationships Native communities had with their traditional foods.
entity_library.June calendar illustration: An illustrated calendar page for June, showing strawberry plants under a red sun, identified by the narrator as marking the beginning of berry season.
entity_library.Historical photograph of acorn storage: A black-and-white photograph of two individuals beside a large woven acorn storage basket in California (c. 1923), part of a sequence illustrating the traditional acorn processing methods of Native peoples, specifically the Southwestern Pomo Indians, who created flour from raw acorns.
entity_library.Historical photograph of baskets used in: A black-and-white photograph showing baskets and acorns used in the preparation of acorn flour in California (c. 1923), part of a sequence illustrating the traditional acorn processing methods of Native peoples, specifically the Southwestern Pomo Indians, who created flour from raw acorns.
entity_library.Acorn processing methods of the Southwes: A close-up video clip showing hands grinding acorns in a bowl, with text overlay, illustrating the traditional processing methods of the Southwestern Pomo Indians of California, who created flour from raw acorns by drying, shelling, smashing, and flushing out bitterness for use in soup, bread, or mush.
entity_library.The Three Sisters illustration: An illustrated diagram on graph paper showing corn, beans, and squash growing together, labeled 'The Three Sisters,' explained by the narrator as crops that 'protected each other and grew better together,' as practiced by nations like the Haudenosaunee and Cherokee.
entity_library.Native Land Dispossession map: A series of maps titled 'Native Land Dispossession,' showing pre-colonization Native land in orange transitioning to a 'Present Day' map with a '99% Reduction' in orange areas, visually representing the land lost to the U.S. due to European colonization over 'just over 300 years,' as narrated.
entity_library.Historical photograph of students in the: A black-and-white photo showing numerous students in formal attire seated at long tables in a large dining hall, captioned 'Students in the dining hall at Carlisle Indian School, Pennsylvania (1901)', framed with colorful geometric borders.
entity_library.Historical photograph of the kitchen at: A black-and-white photo showing three women in aprons standing behind a long table filled with food, captioned 'The kitchen at Carlisle Indian School, Pennsylvania (c. 1895)', framed with colorful geometric borders.
entity_library.Historical photograph of Carlisle Indian: A black-and-white photo showing students tilling soil with tools in a garden, with a building and an adult supervisor in the background, captioned 'Carlisle Indian School students working in a garden, Pennsylvania (c. 1910)', framed with colorful geometric borders.
entity_library.Hand-drawn animation on lined paper: A colorful, hand-drawn illustration on lined paper depicting a Native figure on a blue horse hunting buffalo, with teepees, other figures in traditional attire, and animals in the background, rendered in a childlike, stylized manner.
entity_library.Historical photograph of buffalo herd in: A black-and-white photograph showing a herd of buffalo grazing in a wide, open valley with hills in the background, captioned 'Buffalo herd in the Lamar Valley in Wyoming', framed with colorful geometric borders.
entity_library.Historical photograph of completion of t: A black-and-white photograph showing a large group of men gathered around two trains, with two men shaking hands at the center, captioned 'Completion of the first transcontinental railroad in the U.S., Utah (1869)', framed with colorful geometric borders.
entity_library.Historical photograph of bison skulls: A sepia-toned photograph from 1892 showing a massive pile of bison skulls in Michigan, with men standing at its base and one atop the mound, framed with colorful geometric borders.
entity_library.Historical photograph of men with spruce: A sepia-toned photograph from 1905 showing men posing with a massive spruce log in the Cascade Mountains, Washington, with horses and a log cabin in the background, framed with colorful geometric borders.
entity_library.Historical illustration 'The End' by Mar: A sepia-toned 1883 illustration depicting a desolate landscape littered with bison bones, a wagon, and a lone figure, symbolizing the end of the bison era, framed with colorful geometric borders.
entity_library.Map of Nisqually territory: A green and yellow map of Washington State showing the Nisqually Reservation and the approximate ceded Nisqually territory, with labeled regions and a red triangle marking the reservation, framed with colorful geometric borders.
entity_library.Treaty with the Nisqually, Puyallup, etc: A close-up of the 1854 treaty text, with Article 3 highlighted in orange, stating 'The right of taking fish, at all usual and accustomed grounds and stations, is further secured to said Indians,' framed with colorful geometric borders.
entity_library.Quote from U.S. Army soldier (1867): A text graphic displaying the quote: 'Kill every buffalo you can. Every buffalo dead is an Indian gone.' attributed to a U.S. Army soldier in 1867, set against a textured background with colorful borders.
entity_library.Historical photograph of Muckleshoot pra: A black-and-white photograph from 1970 showing several men wading in the Green River, using long poles to fish, with cars and onlookers visible on the riverbank, illustrating treaty fishing rights in practice.
entity_library.Historical photograph of commercial fish: A black-and-white image from 1906 showing two fishermen on a dock, pulling a large net filled with salmon, with a pile of fish already unloaded on the dock, emphasizing commercial fishing labor.
entity_library.Historical photograph of cannery workers: A black-and-white photograph from 1914 showing two women in aprons and hairnets standing beside a long table filled with rows of canned fish, illustrating industrial food processing.
entity_library.Historical photograph of fifty tons of s: A black-and-white photograph from 1902 showing a massive pile of salmon, emphasizing the scale of commercial fishing in Puget Sound.
entity_library.Native fishers practicing their treaty f: A colorized photograph from 1968–1970 showing a Native fisher in a boat with a 'FISH FIGHT' sign, casting a net, symbolizing resistance and the assertion of treaty rights.
entity_library.Historical photograph of Al Bridges: A black-and-white photo of activist Al Bridges speaking into a bullhorn at a fish-in in Washington (1970), wearing a headband and traditional attire, standing on a vehicle with trees in the background, as the male narrator mentions fish-ins where treaty rights were asserted.
entity_library.Historical photograph of law enforcement: A black-and-white photo showing police vehicles and officers gathering to arrest protestors in Washington (1970), with 'POLICE' written on a car, as the male narrator describes clashes with state police and violence faced by Native fishermen.
entity_library.Historical photograph of Marlon Brando a: A black-and-white photo of actor Marlon Brando (left) and Puyallup activist Bob Satiacum holding fish on the Puyallup River in Washington (1964), with Brando smiling, as the male narrator notes the movement attracted nationwide attention and support from celebrities like Brando.
entity_library.The Boldt Decision graphic: A split-screen graphic with the Man in striped shirt on the left and a text box on the right defining 'The Boldt Decision' as the 1974 court ruling that upheld Treaty Rights and ruled that Native nations in Washington were entitled to half of the harvestable catch of fish, set against a dark textured background with colorful borders, while the male narrator explains this landmark decision.
entity_library.Billy Frank Jr. quote graphic: A graphic displaying the quote: '[I] was not a policy guy... I was a getting-arrested guy.' attributed to Billy Frank Jr., shown alongside a portrait of him in a white shirt with a turquoise necklace, framed with colorful borders, as the male narrator quotes him and describes his frequent arrests.
entity_library.Historical photograph of Navajo on the B: A black-and-white photograph showing Navajo people gathered around horses and buildings on the Bosque Redondo Indian Reservation in New Mexico (c. 1863–1868), framed with colorful geometric borders. This image illustrates the forced relocation of the Navajo Nation, a journey where 'hundreds died' to a reservation in what's now New Mexico.
entity_library.Map of Navajo Homelands and Bosque Redon: A green-toned map showing the Navajo Homelands in orange, with a red wavy line indicating the forced relocation to the Bosque Redondo Reservation and Internment Camp in New Mexico, framed with colorful geometric borders. This map visually represents the 'nearly 500-kilometer journey' mentioned by the speaker.
entity_library.Historical photograph of Corn Fields - B: A black-and-white photograph depicting Navajo people working in corn fields on the Bosque Redondo Indian Reservation in New Mexico (c. 1863–1868), framed with colorful geometric borders. This image illustrates the challenges faced by survivors who found the land 'wasn't good for growing their traditional staples of corn, beans, and squash.'
entity_library.Frybread: Golden-brown, puffy fried bread shown being lifted from a hot oil pan, with a close-up of its crispy texture and golden surface. The man speaking explains it was made from flour, sugar, and lard due to rations, and is seen by some as a reminder of broken traditional food systems leading to health issues, while others view it as a symbol of comfort and resilience.
entity_library.Wyoming PBS footage of making frybread: A modern kitchen scene showing ingredients for frybread (flour, nonfat dry milk, salt, baking powder) on a wooden counter, with a woman mixing them in a blue bowl. Text overlay reads 'Making frybread on the Wind River Indian Reservation Wyoming (2019)' and the Wyoming PBS logo is visible. This footage visually demonstrates the contemporary practice of making frybread, using ingredients like 'white flour' that became part of the diet due to historical circumstances.
entity_library.Frybread / Navajo Taco stand: A small white and blue food stand with a large 'NAVAJO TACO' sign, offering 'HOT FRESH FRY BREAD $5.00', with a customer placing an order at the window.
entity_library.Muckleshoot huckleberry harvest Washingt: A person in a red shirt and woven hat harvesting huckleberries in a forest, with a close-up of the dark purple and red berries in a woven basket. This visual is contextualized by the speaker mentioning the Muckleshoot Food Sovereignty Project, which hosts harvest festivals, maintains community gardens, and teaches tribal members how to prepare salmon and elk.
entity_library.Cherokee traditional salmon smoking Wash: Smoked salmon strips hanging in a smokehouse, with a text overlay indicating the location and year. This visual is linked to the Muckleshoot Food Sovereignty Project's efforts to teach tribal members how to prepare salmon.
entity_library.Cherokee Nation Heirloom Seed Program Ok: Clear plastic bags labeled 'Cherokee White Eagle Corn' and other heirloom seeds, with the Cherokee Nation Administration Support Dept. address printed on the bags. The speaker explains that the Cherokee Nation shares these free heirloom seed packets with their members, encouraging them to grow and eat the same crops as their ancestors.
entity_library.Three Sisters Project at Iowa State Univ: A woman in a blue jacket and black pants standing in a field observing rows of corn, beans, and squash growing together, with close-ups of the plants in the soil. The speaker describes this as the Three Sisters Project at Iowa State University, which partners with native gardeners to study how growing corn, beans, and squash together works.
entity_library.Shawn Sherman, 'The Sioux Chef': A close-up of a person's ear and hair, with on-screen text identifying them as Shawn Sherman, 'The Sioux Chef,' indicating their role as a prominent Indigenous culinary figure.
entity_library.Native American chefs preparing Indigeno: A group of chefs in white uniforms, including one wearing a face mask, standing behind a food preparation counter with ingredients and tools, alongside a woman in a blazer, in a modern kitchen setting.
entity_library.Indigikitchen with Mariah Gladstone: A cooking demonstration showing the steps to make pemmican: blending super dry meat into powder, adding wild rice, pressing into patties, and frying, with on-screen text guiding each step. This demonstration is hosted by Mariah Gladstone, identified by the narrator as a member of the Blackfeet and Cherokee Nations and the host of Indigikitchen, an online cooking show, who shares traditional food knowledge, teaching viewers how to make pemmican and balsamic manoomin with dandelion greens.
entity_library.Traditional Indigenous foods Alaska (201: A buffet scene with hands serving food, including a plate with a bowl of soup and a side of greens, and a large tray of dried fish, set in a rustic wooden interior.
entity_library.Suquamish salmon preparation Washington: Salmon being smoked over a fire using a traditional wooden rack, with flames visible beneath the fish, emphasizing traditional food preparation methods.
entity_library.Dam on the Nisqually River: An aerial view of a large concrete dam spanning the Nisqually River, surrounded by green trees and a road, which the narrator explicitly states still poses threats to the salmon, highlighting its ongoing environmental and cultural impact on Indigenous communities.
